title: Docker
purpose: Package applications and dependencies into reproducible containers.
outcomes: Explain Docker concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important Docker features
Implement, test and debug practical Docker solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of Docker before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Images, containers and registries
topics.definition: A container packages a process and filesystem view while sharing the host kernel. An image is the immutable layered template used to create containers.
topics.whyItMatters: It makes application dependencies and startup behavior reproducible across development, CI and deployment environments.
whenToUse: Package services with controlled runtime dependencies
Create repeatable CI and local environments
Deploy stateless processes under an orchestrator
whenNotToUse: Treating a container as a full virtual machine
Storing durable data only in the writable container layer
Putting secrets into image layers
internalWorking: Image layers provide a content-addressed filesystem
Namespaces isolate process, network and mount views
Cgroups limit and account for resources
The container runtime creates the isolated process from image configuration
tradeoffs: Lightweight process isolation versus sharing the host kernel
Layer caching speeds builds but poor ordering invalidates cache
Reproducibility improves while image security and patching become ongoing work
commonMistakes: Running as root
Using an unpinned oversized base image
Combining build tools and production runtime in one stage
topics.practicalExample: Use a multi-stage build to compile the React client, copy only static output into a minimal runtime image, run as a non-root user and expose a health check.

Images, containers and registries

Requirement and constraints

Validate the input contract
          |
          v
Execute: declarative configuration and controllers move runtime resources toward a desired state
          |

interviewQuestions.question: What is Images, containers and registries?
interviewQuestions.answer: A container packages a process and filesystem view while sharing the host kernel. An image is the immutable layered template used to create containers.
followUps: What changes when the input or traffic for Images, containers and registries grows ten times?

interviewQuestions.question: What problem does Images, containers and registries solve?
interviewQuestions.answer: It makes application dependencies and startup behavior reproducible across development, CI and deployment environments.
followUps: Which failure is most likely in a production Docker implementation?

interviewQuestions.question: When should you use Images, containers and registries?
interviewQuestions.answer: Package services with controlled runtime dependencies Create repeatable CI and local environments Deploy stateless processes under an orchestrator
followUps: When would you choose a simpler alternative to Images, containers and registries?

interviewQuestions.question: When should you avoid Images, containers and registries?
interviewQuestions.answer: Treating a container as a full virtual machine Storing durable data only in the writable container layer Putting secrets into image layers
followUps: What simpler alternative could replace Images, containers and registries, and what would be lost?

interviewQuestions.question: How does Images, containers and registries work internally?
interviewQuestions.answer: Image layers provide a content-addressed filesystem Namespaces isolate process, network and mount views Cgroups limit and account for resources The container runtime creates the isolated process from image configuration
followUps: How would you test and monitor Images, containers and registries?

interviewQuestions.question: What are the main tradeoffs of Images, containers and registries?
interviewQuestions.answer: Lightweight process isolation versus sharing the host kernel Layer caching speeds builds but poor ordering invalidates cache Reproducibility improves while image security and patching become ongoing work
followUps: Which tradeoff matters most under production load?

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Images, containers and registries.
interviewQuestions.answer: An image is an immutable layered filesystem and configuration; a container is an isolated process created from it. Containers share the host kernel and use namespaces and cgroups. I use multi-stage builds, pinned minimal bases, non-root users and external secret injection.
followUps: What changes when the input or traffic for Images, containers and registries grows ten times?
Which failure is most likely in a production Docker implementation?

topics.whyItMatters: It makes application dependencies and startup behavior reproducible across development, CI and deployment environments.
whenToUse: Package services with controlled runtime dependencies
Create repeatable CI and local environments
Deploy stateless processes under an orchestrator
whenNotToUse: Treating a container as a full virtual machine
Storing durable data only in the writable container layer
Putting secrets into image layers
internalWorking: Image layers provide a content-addressed filesystem
Namespaces isolate process, network and mount views
Cgroups limit and account for resources
The container runtime creates the isolated process from image configuration
tradeoffs: Lightweight process isolation versus sharing the host kernel
Layer caching speeds builds but poor ordering invalidates cache
Reproducibility improves while image security and patching become ongoing work
commonMistakes: Running as root
Using an unpinned oversized base image
Combining build tools and production runtime in one stage
topics.practicalExample: Use a multi-stage build to compile the React client, copy only static output into a minimal runtime image, run as a non-root user and expose a health check.

          |

interviewQuestions.question: What are the main tradeoffs of CMD vs ENTRYPOINT?
interviewQuestions.answer: Lightweight process isolation versus sharing the host kernel Layer caching speeds builds but poor ordering invalidates cache Reproducibility improves while image security and patching become ongoing work

interviewQuestions.answer: An image is an immutable layered filesystem and configuration; a container is an isolated process created from it. Containers share the host kernel and use namespaces and cgroups. I use multi-stage builds, pinned minimal bases, non-root users and external secret injection.
